comments:
Summary
This paper introduces a model that treats amicus briefs as outputs of utility-maximizing agents and incorporates them into an ideal-point (IP) framework grounded by topic models. The authors propose a random-utility prior over amicus topic mixtures, combine LDA-derived representations of merits and amicus briefs with multidimensional IPs for justices, and estimate parameters via a two-stage approach (LDA then MCMC for IP parameters). Empirical evaluation on SCOTUS data (1990–2012) shows improved vote partition prediction relative to naïve baselines and enables counterfactual analyses (e.g., NFIB v. Sebelius) and diagnostics about amici influence.

Strengths
- Clear and well-motivated research question: modeling persuasive text as the byproduct of agents with goals is conceptually appealing and relevant to political science and NLP.
- A novel combination of established methods (ideal points, LDA, random utility) tailored to the amici/SCOTUS setting.
- Careful description of model components, sampling/inference procedure, and hyperparameters; discussion acknowledges simplifying assumptions.
- Empirical results are interesting: the random-utility model improves predictive accuracy and enables interpretable counterfactuals and per-justice influence diagnostics.
- Good qualitative analyses (case-level plots, expected-utility curves) that illustrate the model’s affordances.

Weaknesses and concerns
- Simplifying assumptions limit realism. The authors themselves note (i) amici are modeled independently (no strategic interactions), (ii) briefs on a side are averaged into a single ∆ and weighted equally, (iii) cost and utility functions are simple and may not capture real amicus incentives, and (iv) amici are assumed to know justices’ IPs. These restrict causal claims about influence.
- Treatment of amicus-side labeling: briefs were auto-classified and only high-confidence examples were used (7,258 of 13,162). The classifier accuracy and the selection of high-confidence briefs could bias results; more analysis of the impact of labeling errors/sampling bias is needed.
- Decoupling LDA from IP estimation (two-stage approach) is pragmatic but may miss benefits of joint inference; the paper notes similar dimensions in preliminary experiments, but more systematic evidence would strengthen the claim.
- Evaluation metric: because of symmetry in the model, the main reported metric is pairwise partition accuracy rather than direct vote accuracy or calibrated probability scores. Additional standard metrics (per-justice accuracy, log-likelihood, Brier score, calibration plots) would help quantify gains and practical utility.
- Baselines and ablations: comparisons include naïve baselines and a regularized logistic regression using topic features. It would be useful to compare against Lauderdale & Clark (issues-only IP) more directly (as an explicit baseline), and to perform ablation studies isolating the contribution of the utility prior (η) and of including amici aggregate ∆ vs. individual briefs.
- Sensitivity analyses are limited. The impact of hyperparameters (η, ξ, number of topics) and of using different thresholds for brief-side classifier confidence should be explored.

Suggestions for improvement
- Provide additional quantitative evaluations: per-justice accuracy, overall vote accuracy, log-likelihood, and calibration measures; report statistical significance and effect sizes versus stronger baselines.
- Include ablation studies: (a) issues-only vs. issues+amici without the utility prior vs. full random-utility model, (b) using individual-brief ∆k vs. side-aggregated ∆s, and (c) varying η and ξ to demonstrate robustness.
- Analyze the effect of the brief-side classifier: show results on (i) gold-labeled subset, (ii) all auto-labeled briefs, and (iii) the high-confidence subset used, to quantify any selection bias.
- Discuss feasibility and implications of modeling interactions among amici (e.g., adversarial framing), as a limitation and avenue for future work.
- Consider releasing code and labeled subsets (as permitted) to facilitate replication and follow-up studies.

Overall assessment
This paper presents a thoughtful and original contribution to modeling persuasive text in a substantive, high-impact domain. The methods are sound and well-explained, and the empirical examples are compelling. The work would be strengthened by more extensive evaluation, ablations, and sensitivity checks, and by a deeper analysis of the impact of the brief-side classification and selection. I recommend acceptance (strongly favorable).
